question: Are there Consequential Damages on the Project?
raw_answer: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract.

question: What are the are the Consequential Damages Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract. This mutual waiver includes, without limitation: damages incurred by the Owner for additional interest, penalties, taxes, insurance, additional marketing or management costs, rental expenses, for losses of use, income, profit, financing, business and reputation to Owner or to the Project, and for loss of management or employee productivity or of the services of such persons and for lost or deferred sales, rents or use of property including the Project or portions thereof or reduced sales or rent prices or diminution in value, and damages incurred by the Contractor for principal office expenses including the compensation of personnel stationed there, for losses of financing, business and reputation, and for loss of profit except anticipated profit arising directly from the Work. This mutual waiver is applicable, without limitation, to all consequential damages due to either party's termination of the Contract as provided in the Contract Documents. Nothing contained in this Section 4.3.10 shall be deemed to preclude an award of agreed liquidated damages in accordance with the requirements of the Agreement.

raw_answer: The Owner shall have the right to require the Contractor to furnish bonds, subject to Owner’s approval, which cover Contractor’s faithful performance of the Contract and payment of obligations arising thereunder.

question: What is the Payment & Performance Bond Article #?
What are the Payment & Performance Bond Article Terms & Conditions?
value: The Owner has the right to require the Contractor to furnish bonds that cover the Contractor’s faithful performance of the Contract and payment of obligations arising thereunder. Upon the Owner's request, the Contractor must purchase and maintain a Performance and Labor and Materials Bond executed by a Surety with a minimum rating of A.M. Best A, in favor of the Owner, on a standard AIA bond form, A312. This bond must be in an amount equal to 100% of the Contract Price and must remain in full force and effect through the warranty and extended warranty periods. The Contractor must provide a copy of the bonds to any potential beneficiary upon request.

question: What is the IMC Design Document Review obligations Article #?
raw_answer: Article 7

question: What are the Terms & Conditions / IMC's obligations for Design Document Review?
value: The Contractor is obligated to carefully study and compare the various Drawings and other Contract Documents before starting the Work and each portion of the Work. The Contractor must take field measurements of any existing conditions related to that portion of the Work and observe any conditions at the site affecting it. Any errors, inconsistencies, omissions, or problems with construction feasibility discovered must be immediately reported to the Owner and the Architect in writing. The Contractor must review for compliance with the Contract Documents, approve, and submit to the Architect Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples, and similar submittals required by the Contract Documents with reasonable promptness and in such sequence as to cause no delay in the Work or in the activities of the Owner or of separate contractors. The Contractor represents that all dimensions, quantities, field dimensions, relations to existing structures and work, coordination with work to be installed later, coordination with information on previously accepted Shop Drawings, Product Data, or Samples, and verification of compliance with all the requirements of the Contract Documents have been determined and verified. The Contractor shall perform no portion of the Work for which the Contract Documents require submittal and review of Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples, or similar submittals until the respective submittal has been approved by the Architect.
raw_answer: The Contractor must study and compare Drawings and Contract Documents, take field measurements, observe site conditions, and report any issues to the Owner and Architect. The Contractor must review, approve, and submit Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples, and similar submittals for compliance with the Contract Documents and ensure no delay in the Work.

question: What is the Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Article #?
What are the Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contractor must inform the Architect and the Owner in writing of any proposed deviations from the detailed requirements of the Contract Documents at the time the material is submitted for approval. The Contractor must request written approval for such deviations. When requesting approval for deviations or substitutions, the Contractor must provide evidence that the proposed change will deliver a quality of result at least equal to the original specifications. If the Architect or Owner finds the evidence insufficient, they may reject the substitution or deviation. If a deviation or substitution is approved, the Contractor is responsible for any additional work and materials required as a result, at no cost to the Owner and without delaying the project. Approval of a substitution does not relieve the Contractor from liability for non-compliance with the Contract Documents. The Architect has a responsibility to review and incorporate Architect/Owner-requested substitutions into the design. The Contractor is also required to maintain a record copy of all relevant documents and submittals at the site, which must be available to the Architect and Owner during the project and delivered to the Architect upon completion of the work.

question: What is the Subcontractor Bid Requirements Article #?

question: What is the Subcontractor Required Number of Bids per package?
What are the Subcontractor Bids / Bid Tabs Provided to Owner Terms & Conditions?
value: If a specific bidder among those whose bids are delivered by the Contractor to the Architect (1) is recommended to the Owner by the Contractor; (2) is qualified to perform that portion of the Work; and (3) has submitted a bid that conforms to the requirements of the Contract Documents without reservations or exceptions, but the Owner requires that another bid be accepted, then the Contractor may require that a Change Order be issued to adjust the Guaranteed Maximum Price by the difference between the bid of the person or entity recommended to the Owner by the Contractor and the amount of the subcontract or other agreement actually signed with the person or entity designated by the Owner. Subcontracts or other agreements shall conform to the applicable payment provisions of this Agreement and the Contract Documents generally, and shall not be awarded on the basis of cost plus a fee without the prior written consent of the Owner. The Contractor shall provide the Owner with copies of all subcontracts within thirty (30) days of execution thereof.
raw_answer: Subcontractor bids must be recommended by the Contractor, qualified, and conform to the Contract Documents. If the Owner requires another bid, a Change Order may adjust the Guaranteed Maximum Price. Subcontracts must conform to payment provisions and not be cost plus a fee without Owner's consent. Copies of all subcontracts must be provided to the Owner within 30 days of execution.

question: What are the GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions?
value: The GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions are outlined in the provided excerpts. The Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) is defined as the total maximum cost to be paid by the Owner for the Contractor’s full and complete performance under the Contract Documents, including all services, labor, materials, equipment, and fees. The Value Management (VM) Target Costs include all markup and insurances, and VM item savings do not impact the IMC Fee total. Target savings values assume that clarification and reconciliation of all line items and final costs are complete and approved within 90 days of GMP Agreement execution. If target savings are not achieved, the owner will execute an add/deduct change order to IMC for the change from the target number. The additional VM scope information is incorporated for reference only to establish a baseline understanding of intended VM scope target values. Current design documentation shall be utilized for final VM savings.

question: What is the Contingency Usage Article #?
What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
value: If, after consultation between the Owner and the Contractor, the Owner requests that the General Contractor default a Subcontractor and the Contractor refuses to default such Subcontractor, the Contractor shall, at its sole cost and expense and without the use of the Contingency, be responsible for all costs, damages and delays arising from such Subcontractor’s default or failure to perform. If after consultation between the Owner and Contractor, the Owner requests that the Contractor default a Subcontractor and the Contractor elects not to default such Subcontractor for default of performance, the Contingency may only be used if the Contractor demonstrates to Owner, in Owner’s reasonable discretion, that continued performance by the Subcontractor is in the best interests of the Project, including prosecution of the Work in accordance with the Project Schedule, relative to utilizing a replacement subcontractor and/or defaulting the subcontractor and allowing them to remain on the job while the claim is pending with the SDI carrier(s), and Owner agrees to same in writing.
raw_answer: Contingency may only be used if the Contractor demonstrates to Owner that continued performance by the Subcontractor is in the best interests of the Project and Owner agrees in writing.

What is the Adverse Weather Days Article #?
What are the Adverse Weather Days Terms & Conditions?
value: If adverse weather conditions are the basis for a Claim for additional time, such Claim shall be documented by data substantiating the weather conditions were abnormal for the period of time as compared with the ten-year average for the local geographical area where the Project is situated, could not have been reasonably anticipated and had an adverse effect on the critical path schedule. The Contractor assumes the risks of the normal weather patterns of the local area in which the Project is located. No claim for a time extension shall be allowed on account of a failure of the Architect to furnish interpretations, instructions or drawings or to return Shop Drawings or samples until ten (10) workdays after receipt by the Architect of a written request for such instructions, drawings, or samples and only if such delay impacts the Project’s critical path and Contractor has complied with the notice and proof provisions set forth in Article 4. Contractor shall not be allowed an extension of time unless Contractor has established to the Owner’s satisfaction that the delay claimed by the Contractor is to a portion of the Work on the critical path of the Contract Schedule, and the Project is currently behind the current Contract Schedule.
raw_answer: Adverse weather conditions must be abnormal compared to the ten-year average, could not have been anticipated, and must affect the critical path schedule. The Contractor assumes normal weather risks. Claims for time extensions due to adverse weather must be documented and meet specific criteria.

question: Within how many days must we notify the owner of adverse weather day impacts?
raw_answer: ten (10) workdays

question: How many Adverse Weather Days Included in Contract Agreement?
What is the Schedule Requirements Article #?
What are the Schedule Requirements Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contract Schedule shall be based on an orderly progression of the Work, allowing adequate time for each operation, and leading to Substantial Completion by the date(s) established in the Agreement. The Contractor must coordinate its work with the work of other third-party contractors. The Contract Schedule will be reviewed by the Owner and the Architect for compliance with the requirements of this Article and will be accepted by them only in the form of an approved Change Order. Schedules submitted or used by the Contractor that are not the current Contract Schedule accepted in an approved Change Order are for the sole use of the Contractor, at his risk, and do not relate to this Contract. Unless specifically required by law, no initial payment under this Contract shall be due until the Contract Schedule has been approved by the Owner and the Architect. If the Contractor and Owner have agreed that the Contractor should be permitted to extend the time for completion as provided in Subparagraph 4.3.7, the calendar dates in the Contract Schedule shall be adjusted accordingly to retain their same relationship to the adjusted date of Substantial Completion, and the dollar value of work to be completed as the first of each month shall be adjusted pro-rata. The Contractor shall furnish sufficient forces, plant and equipment and shall work such hours, including night shifts and lawful overtime operations as may be necessary to ensure the prosecution of the work in accordance with the Contract Schedule. If, in the opinion of the Owner, the contractor falls behind schedule, the Contractor must take such steps as may be necessary to improve his progress, and must submit his plans demonstrating the manner in which the desired rate of progress may be regained. It shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to maintain his schedule so as not to delay the progress of the Work or the scheduled work of other persons. If Contractor delays the progress of the Work, in order to regain the time lost and to maintain the Contract Schedule, it shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to increase the number of shifts, days of Work, and/or to the extent permitted by law, to institute or increase overtime operations, all without additional cost to the Owner in excess of the GMP.
raw_answer: The Contract Schedule shall be based on an orderly progression of the Work, allowing adequate time for each operation, and leading to Substantial Completion by the date(s) established in the Agreement.

question: What is the Daily Log & Manpower Article #?
What are the Daily Log & Manpower Terms and Conditions?
What is the Schedule Delay / Time Issues Article #?
What are the Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions?
value: The Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions are detailed in several sections of the contract. If adverse weather conditions are the basis for a claim for additional time, the claim must be documented with data showing that the weather conditions were abnormal for the period compared to the ten-year average for the local area, could not have been reasonably anticipated, and had an adverse effect on the critical path schedule. No claim for a time extension shall be allowed due to the failure of the Architect to furnish interpretations, instructions, or drawings until ten workdays after receipt of a written request, and only if the delay impacts the project's critical path. The Contractor must establish to the Owner's satisfaction that the delay is to a portion of the work on the critical path and that the project is currently behind schedule. If the Contractor wishes to make a claim for an increase in the contract time, written notice must be given, including an estimate of the probable effect of the delay on the progress of the work. The Contractor is entitled to an extension of time equal to the actual time lost on the critical path due to specific causes such as failure to act, direction, order, neglect, delay, or default of the Owner, the Architect/Engineer, or any other contractor employed on the project; changes in the work; fire, lightning, earthquake, enemy action, act of God, epidemic or pandemic, or similar catastrophe; government restrictions; or labor disputes beyond the Contractor's reasonable control. The Contractor waives any claim for damages on account of any delay, obstruction, or hindrance for any cause whatsoever and agrees that the sole remedy for such delays is an extension of time, provided written notice of the claim has been met. If the Contractor incurs actual proven and incurred additional costs due to a delay to the critical path beyond the Contractor's control, and such costs are detailed in writing and supported with satisfactory documentation, the Contractor is entitled to present a claim for those costs.
raw_answer: The Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions include provisions for claims due to adverse weather, failure of the Architect to provide necessary documents, and other specific causes. The Contractor must provide written notice and documentation for claims, and the sole remedy for delays is an extension of time, with the possibility of claiming additional costs if properly documented.

question: How much Schedule Delay / Time Issues Notice to Owner Requirements (Within What # of Days)?
raw_answer: ten (10) workdays

question: What is the Schedule Acceleration Directed by Owner Article #?
What are the Schedule Acceleration Directed by Owner Terms & Conditions?
value: In order to expedite the completion of the Work, the Owner, in his sole discretion, may direct the Contractor to increase the number of shifts, days of work, and/or perform overtime operations or to take such other measures as he deems necessary to expedite construction. Such instruction shall be in writing and signed by the Owner. The Contractor, however, shall not be entitled to any additional costs unless the need for such direction was not attributable to the actions, omissions, delays or other fault of the Contractor. In such an event, the Owner shall pay the Contractor the additional cost as mutually agreed upon in writing. If the Contractor is behind schedule due to his own actions, omissions, delays or other fault on its part and the Owner directs the Contractor to work additional shifts, overtime, expedite deliveries, supplement its forces or purchase additional materials or equipment in order to bring his work up to schedule, all additional cost in excess of the GMP shall be borne by the Contractor.
raw_answer: Owner may direct Contractor to expedite work through additional shifts, days, or overtime. Costs are covered by Owner if delays are not Contractor's fault; otherwise, Contractor bears the cost.

question: What are the Schedule Definitions of Days (Work / Calendar / Holidays / Etc.)?
value: The term 'day' as used in the Contract Documents shall mean calendar day unless otherwise specifically defined. The term 'working day' shall mean any calendar day except Saturdays, Sundays and Legal Holidays at the place of the Project.
raw_answer: The term 'day' as used in the Contract Documents shall mean calendar day unless otherwise specifically defined. The term 'working day' shall mean any calendar day except Saturdays, Sundays and Legal Holidays at the place of the Project.

question: What is the Substantial Completion Article #?
What are the Substantial Completion Terms & Conditions?
value: The Substantial Completion terms and conditions are as follows: Substantial Completion is the stage when all of the Work is sufficiently complete in accordance with the Contract Documents and all Work under this Contract required to license the building is complete to allow for a licensing inspection by a licensing agency so that the Owner can occupy and utilize the Work for its intended use. However, Substantial Completion will not be reached until all Work under this Contract is completed to allow for necessary licenses, Certificates of Occupancy are issued by the appropriate governmental authorities, all finishes are properly installed, all as-built drawings, Operations and Maintenance manuals and warranties have been provided to the Owner, and the Architect has issued a Certificate of Substantial Completion. When the Contractor considers that the Work or a portion thereof is substantially complete, the Contractor shall submit to the Architect and the Owner a list of items to be completed or corrected and all special warranties required by the Contract Documents. The Architect will then make an inspection to determine whether the Work is substantially complete. Warranties required by the Contract Documents shall commence on the date of Substantial Completion unless otherwise provided in the Certificate of Substantial Completion. The Contractor must notify the Architect that the Work is ready for final inspection and acceptance on or before the expiration of forty-five days from the date of Substantial Completion. If the Contractor fails to notify the Architect within this period, the Owner has the right to cure and complete the Work at the expense of the Contractor.
raw_answer: Substantial Completion terms and conditions include completion of all work for licensing inspection, issuance of Certificates of Occupancy, installation of all finishes, provision of as-built drawings, manuals, and warranties, and issuance of a Certificate of Substantial Completion by the Architect. The Contractor must notify the Architect within 45 days for final inspection.

question: What are the Payment Terms Retainage %?
What is the Payment Terms for Retainage Reduction Article #?
What are the Payment Terms & Conditions for Retainage Reduction?
value: The Owner shall withhold as retainage 10% of each approved progress payment through 50% completion, on a line item basis, of the Project. Once a line item reaches 50% completion no further retainage will be held on that line item. This retainage shall be withheld until Substantial Completion as defined in the Contract Documents. Upon Substantial Completion of the Work by the Contractor, the Contractor shall be paid a sum sufficient to increase the total payments to the Contractor to 100% of the Cost of the Work and Fees, but less an amount equal to 200% of the costs of the Work for incomplete Work or Punch List Work. However, in no event will the retainage be reduced lower than the amount required by the Lender in the Construction Loan Agreement. In the event of any inconsistency between this retainage clause and the Construction Loan Agreement, the Construction Loan Agreement shall govern.
raw_answer: The Owner shall withhold as retainage 10% of each approved progress payment through 50% completion, on a line item basis, of the Project. Once a line item reaches 50% completion no further retainage will be held on that line item. This retainage shall be withheld until Substantial Completion as defined in the Contract Documents. Upon Substantial Completion of the Work by the Contractor, the Contractor shall be paid a sum sufficient to increase the total payments to the Contractor to 100% of the Cost of the Work and Fees, but less an amount equal to 200% of the costs of the Work for incomplete Work or Punch List Work. However, in no event will the retainage be reduced lower than the amount required by the Lender in the Construction Loan Agreement. In the event of any inconsistency between this retainage clause and the Construction Loan Agreement, the Construction Loan Agreement shall govern.

question: What is the Payment Terms for Offsite Stored Material Article #?

question: What are the Terms and Conditions for Payment of Offsite Stored Material?
value: Payments for materials and equipment stored offsite may be made if approved in advance by the Owner and stored at a location agreed upon in writing. Payment is conditioned upon the Contractor's compliance with procedures satisfactory to the Owner to establish the Owner's title to such materials and equipment or otherwise protect the Owner's interest. This includes the costs of applicable insurance, storage, and transportation to the site. The Contractor is responsible for any loss or damage to unincorporated materials or equipment stored offsite not covered by insurance.

Payment is conditioned upon the Contractor's compliance with procedures satisfactory to the Owner to establish the Owner's title to such materials and equipment or otherwise protect the Owner's interest. This includes the costs of applicable insurance, storage, and transportation to the site. The Contractor is responsible for any loss or damage to unincorporated materials or equipment stored offsite not covered by insurance.

raw_answer: 30 days

question: What is the Article # for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
value: If an undisputed amount is due to the Contractor and not paid by the Owner within forty-five (45) days after receipt of the Contractor's Application for Payment, or if the Owner does not pay the Contractor within seven (7) days after the date established in the Contract Documents the amount certified by the Architect, the Contractor may, upon seven (7) additional days' written notice to the Owner and Architect, stop the Work until payment of the amount owing has been received.
raw_answer: Contractor may stop work if payment is not received within specified timeframes.

What is the Warranty Terms & Conditions Article #?
What are the Warranty Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contractor warrants to the Owner that materials and equipment furnished under the Contract will be of good quality and new unless otherwise required or permitted by the Contract Documents, that the Work will be free from defects not inherent in the quality required or permitted, and that the Work will conform to the requirements of the Contract Documents. Work not conforming to these requirements, including substitutions not properly approved and authorized, shall be considered defective. The Contractor's warranty excludes remedy for damage or defect caused by abuse, modifications not executed by the Contractor, improper or insufficient maintenance, improper operation, or normal wear and tear and normal usage. If required by the Architect, the Contractor shall furnish satisfactory evidence as to the kind and quality of materials and equipment. The warranty provided by the Contractor shall be in addition to and not in limitation of any other warranty required by the Contract Documents or otherwise prescribed by law. The Contractor shall procure and deliver to the Architect, no later than the date claimed by the Contractor as the date of Substantial Completion of the Work, all special warranties required by the Contract Documents. Delivery by the Contractor shall constitute the Contractor’s guarantee to the Owner that the warranty will be performed in accordance with its terms and conditions. All warranties applicable to the Work begin as of the date of Substantial Completion of the Work. The Contractor agrees to assign to the Owner at the time of Substantial Completion of the Project Work any and all manufacturer’s warranties relating to materials and labor used in the Work and, to the extent not otherwise required by the Contract Documents, further agrees to perform the Work in such manner so as to preserve any and all such manufacturer’s warranties. Such assignment does not relieve the Contractor of its responsibility to correct all work during the warranty period. The Contractor warrants that all manufacturers’ or other warranties on all materials and equipment furnished by the Contractor shall run directly to or be specifically assigned to the Owner on demand or upon final completion of the Project.
raw_answer: The Contractor warrants to the Owner that materials and equipment furnished under the Contract will be of good quality and new unless otherwise required or permitted by the Contract Documents, that the Work will be free from defects not inherent in the quality required or permitted, and that the Work will conform to the requirements of the Contract Documents.

question: What is the As-Built / Record Documents Requirements Article #?
What are the As-Built / Record Documents Requirements?
value: The Contractor is required to maintain at the site one record copy of the Drawings, Specifications, Addenda, Change Orders, and other Modifications, marked currently to record field changes and selections made during construction. This record copy must include architectural and structural changes, plumbing, heating, ventilating and air conditioning changes, electrical changes, and changes to site work including grades and utilities. Additionally, the Contractor must maintain a Record set of all meeting minutes and a Daily Log throughout the duration of the Project. These documents must be available to the Architect and the Owner during the Project and delivered to the Architect for submittal to the Owner upon completion of the Work. If required by the Lender, 'As-Built' drawings and specifications that include all modifications and changes must also be provided.
raw_answer: The Contractor is required to maintain at the site one record copy of the Drawings, Specifications, Addenda, Change Orders, and other Modifications, marked currently to record field changes and selections made during construction. This record copy must include architectural and structural changes, plumbing, heating, ventilating and air conditioning changes, electrical changes, and changes to site work including grades and utilities. Additionally, the Contractor must maintain a Record set of all meeting minutes and a Daily Log throughout the duration of the Project. These documents must be available to the Architect and the Owner during the Project and delivered to the Architect for submittal to the Owner upon completion of the Work. If required by the Lender, 'As-Built' drawings and specifications that include all modifications and changes must also be provided.

question: What is the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Article #?
What are the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Terms & Conditions?
value: If reasonable precautions will be inadequate to prevent foreseeable bodily injury or death to persons resulting from a material or substance, including but not limited to asbestos or polychlorinated biphenyl (PCB), encountered on the site by the Contractor, the Contractor shall, upon recognizing the condition, immediately stop Work in the affected area and report the condition to the Owner and Architect in writing. The Contractor shall have no responsibility for the discovery, presence, handling, removal, disposal or exposure of persons to hazardous substances existing in any form at the site unless the Contractor or anyone for whom Contractor is liable introduces the hazardous substance to the site, or unless the Contract Documents require the abatement and removal of said hazardous substances. The Owner shall obtain the services of a licensed laboratory to verify the presence or absence of the material or substance reported by the Contractor and, in the event such material or substance is found to be present, to verify that it has been rendered harmless. Unless otherwise required by the Contract Documents, the Owner shall furnish in writing to the Contractor and Architect the names and qualifications of persons or entities who are to perform tests verifying the presence or absence of such material or substance or who are to perform the task of removal or safe containment of such material or substance. The Contractor and the Architect will promptly reply to the Owner in writing.
raw_answer: Contractor must stop work and report hazardous conditions; Owner handles verification and removal.
